# Project plan

## Start-up phase
<!--
Covering the planning phase, this section should provide a relatively
detailed plan of how work will start on the project. This section is
important because projects need to get up and running quickly.


 - [ ] Setting up collaboration platform inc. code for contributors etc.
 - [ ] Licence decisions
 - [ ] Reporting framework
-->

During the start-up phase, the project will establish the foundation for open, modular, and mentor-reviewable development.

The initial work will include:

- Set up or reorganize the GitHub repository structure for skills, tools, examples, tests, and documentation.
- Confirm the project license and contribution guidelines.
- Define the initial `SKILL.md` format and folder structure.
- Confirm the first set of high-priority skills with mentors.
- Define the MCP tool interface and expected input/output format.
- Create a simple reporting framework using GitHub issues, milestones, and weekly progress notes.
- Identify a small set of representative R tasks for ad hoc mentor evaluation.
- Prepare the initial roadmap and public project description.

The expected outcome of this phase is a clear and usable development foundation: an organized repository, a confirmed scope, a public roadmap, and a shared understanding of what will be delivered first.


## Technical delivery
<!--
Covering the actual delivery of the project this section should
provide at least a high-level description of the implementation.

Including target dates is really important as you need to be committed
and the ISC need to have a means of tracking delivery
-->

The technical delivery will focus on building reusable R skills and MCP-wrapped tools in small, testable milestones.

- **M1 – Repository and First Skill Draft (Weeks 1–2):**  
  Create the base repository structure, define the `SKILL.md` template, draft the Expert End-to-End Data Analyst skill, and implement an initial R session introspection function.

- **M2 – Session and Dataset Introspection MVP (Weeks 3–4):**  
  Implement tools for inspecting the current R session and datasets, including loaded packages, objects, object classes, dataset dimensions, variable names, data types, missingness, and simple summaries.

- **M3 – MCP Wrapping and Example Workflow (Weeks 5–6):**  
  Wrap the stable introspection functions as MCP tools or MCP-compatible interfaces. Add at least one realistic example workflow showing how an agent can use the Data Analyst skill and tools to understand an R analysis context.

- **M4 – Local Package Library Inspection (Weeks 7–8):**  
  Implement local package inspection functionality, including installed packages, versions, exported functions, documentation availability, and basic dependency information. Begin the R Package Developer skill.

- **M5 – CRAN Package Index Query and Workflow Integration (Weeks 9–10):**  
  Implement a CRAN package metadata query tool and connect session, dataset, local package, and CRAN context into more complete example workflows.

- **M6 – Documentation, Testing, and Final Demo (Weeks 11–12):**  
  Polish documentation, setup instructions, tests, example tasks, and final demo materials. Refine skills and tools based on mentor feedback and prepare the final report.


**Progress tracking:** Progress will be tracked through GitHub issues, milestones, weekly update notes, and regular mentor reviews. Each milestone will produce a concrete deliverable that can be inspected, tested, or reviewed.



## Other aspects

<!--
How will you ensure that your work is available to the widest number
of people? Please specify the open-source or creative commons license(s)
you will use, how you will host your code so that others can contribute,
and how you will publicize your work. We encourage you to plan content
to be shared quarterly on the R Consortium blog.

 - [ ] Announcement post
 - [ ] Delivery blog post
 - [ ] Social media
 - [ ] UseR!
 - [ ] ISC meetings
-->

The project will be developed openly on GitHub so that mentors, R users, package developers, and future contributors can inspect progress and contribute feedback.

The project will use an open-source license such as MIT or GPL, depending on mentor and community guidance. Documentation and examples will be written clearly so that users can understand each skill and tool independently.

Community-facing activities may include:

- A short announcement post introducing the project goals and roadmap.
- Regular GitHub updates showing weekly progress.
- Example-driven documentation for each skill and tool.
- A final delivery post summarizing the completed work and future directions.
- Sharing the project with R community channels, relevant open-source communities, and possible R Consortium updates.

The project is designed to be reusable beyond one interface or one model. By focusing on `SKILL.md` files and MCP-wrapped tools, the outputs can be adapted by different agentic IDEs and future R assistant projects.



## Budget & funding plan

<!--
Outline the milestones for development and how much funding will be
required for each stage (as payments will be tied to project milestone
completion). Each milestone should specify the work to be done and the
expected outcomes, providing enough detail for the ISC to understand
the scope of the project work and assess the likelihood of success.

We expect that most of the budget will be allocated
for labor costs. We do not cover indirect costs. The ISC grants cannot
cover such things as travel, lodging, food, journal publication fees,
or personal hardware. Cloud services may be covered if they are specific
to the project and the project period. The ISC reserves the right to vet
how funds are used for each project separately. If in doubt, please reach
out to us.
-->

Total request: **$3,000**, allocated primarily to development labor, documentation, testing, and demo preparation.

The funding will support:

- Designing and implementing reusable R skills.
- Developing MCP-wrapped tools for R session, dataset, package, and CRAN introspection.
- Creating documentation, usage examples, and setup instructions.
- Preparing representative evaluation tasks and final demo materials.
- Maintaining the GitHub repository and weekly progress updates.

Most of the budget will be used for developer labor. No funds are requested for travel, lodging, food, journal publication fees, or personal hardware.

**Milestone-based funding plan**:  

| Milestone | Timeline | Work to be Done | Expected Outcome | Budget |
|---|---:|---|---|---:|
| M1: Repository and First Skill Draft | Weeks 1–2 | Set up repository structure, define `SKILL.md` template, draft first skill, begin session introspection | Organized repo, first skill draft, initial tool prototype | 15% |
| M2: Session and Dataset Introspection MVP | Weeks 3–4 | Build session and dataset introspection tools | Working MVP for exposing R session and dataset context | 20% |
| M3: MCP Wrapping and Example Workflow | Weeks 5–6 | Wrap core tools with MCP-compatible interfaces and add example workflow | Agent-usable workflow demonstrating context-aware R assistance | 20% |
| M4: Local Package Library Inspection | Weeks 7–8 | Build package inspection tool and begin R Package Developer skill | Tool for installed package/version/documentation awareness | 15% |
| M5: CRAN Query and Workflow Integration | Weeks 9–10 | Build CRAN metadata query tool and integrate multiple context sources | More complete workflow for package-aware R assistance | 15% |
| M6: Documentation, Testing, and Final Demo | Weeks 11–12 | Polish docs, examples, tests, demo, and final report | Final documented release and mentor-reviewable demo | 15% |

This milestone-based plan keeps the project realistic and measurable. Instead of promising a complete production assistant or CRAN release in the initial period, the project focuses on reusable infrastructure that can support future ChatR development.
